paper.title: Modeling the effect of water activity on dairy microflora growth in cream cheese

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: Full Baranyi primary growth model incorporating both a lag phase adjustment function and logistic saturation. The lag is modeled through an adjustment function A(t) that depends on the initial physiological state of the cells.
assumptions: Lag phase is modeled via an adjustment function A(t) = t + (1/mu) * ln(exp(-mu*t) + exp(-h0) - exp(-mu*t - h0)).
Parameter h0 represents the initial physiological state of the cells.

source_spans.text_context: Full Baranyi primary growth model incorporating both a lag phase adjustment function and logistic saturation. The lag is modeled through an adjustment function A(t) that depends on the initial physiological state of the cells.

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: Empirical square-root model relating the square root of growth rate to temperature, widely used in predictive microbiology as a simple secondary model.
assumptions: Linear relationship between sqrt(mu) and temperature in the suboptimal range.
T_min is a conceptual (extrapolated) value, not necessarily the observed minimum.

source_spans.text_context: Empirical square-root model relating the square root of growth rate to temperature, widely used in predictive microbiology as a simple secondary model.

mechanistic_equations.equation_description: Logistic primary growth model for bacterial concentration over time, assuming no lag phase. The population grows from an initial level toward a maximum carrying capacity with growth rate governed by environmental conditions.

variables.name: time
variables.description: elapsed time since start of growth

variables.name: population at time t
variables.description: logarithmic concentration of the target organism at time t

assumptions: No lag phase; growth begins immediately at the given rate.
Logistic growth with a fixed maximum population density.

source_spans.text_context: Logistic primary growth model for bacterial concentration over time, assuming no lag phase. The population grows from an initial level toward a maximum carrying capacity with growth rate governed by environmental conditions.
